title: APIs & Communication
description: Designing service interfaces: protocols, contracts, real-time delivery and gateways.

questions.question: Compare REST and gRPC as communication styles between services. What properties of each would push you toward one or the other?
What makes an API change backward-compatible vs breaking, and what strategies exist for evolving an API that external clients already depend on?
Offset-based pagination (page=5&size=20) misbehaves on large, frequently-changing datasets. Why, and how does cursor (keyset) pagination fix it?
questions.explanation:
Intuition: page=5&size=20 means "skip 100 rows, give me the next 20". Two things go wrong: the database must still walk and discard those 100 rows (at page 5000 that's 100,000 rows scanned for 20 returned — cost grows with the offset), and the offset counts whatever the data looks like right now, so concurrent inserts/deletes shift the pages under the user.

-- user is reading page 5 (rows 101-120), sorted newest-first
-- meanwhile 3 new rows are inserted at the top
-- 'page=6' now skips 120 rows of the SHIFTED list:
--   3 rows the user already saw appear again (duplicates)
-- if 3 rows were DELETED instead, 3 unseen rows are silently skipped

Cursor (keyset) pagination replaces "skip N" with "continue after the last row I saw". The server returns an opaque cursor encoding the sort-key values of the last item; the next request filters on it:

SELECT * FROM posts
WHERE (created_at, id) < (:last_created_at, :last_id)  -- resume after cursor
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 20;
  • Fast: with an index on (created_at, id) the DB seeks straight to the cursor — no rows scanned and thrown away, page 1 and page 5000 cost the same.
  • Stable: the anchor is a concrete row, not a count — inserts/deletes elsewhere can't cause duplicates or skips.
  • Tie-breaker matters: id is added because created_at alone isn't unique — a non-unique cursor can skip or repeat rows on the boundary.

The trade-off: you lose "jump to page 37" and total page counts — you can only walk forward (or backward with a reversed cursor). That's why keyset fits infinite-scroll feeds and APIs (GitHub, Stripe, Slack all use cursors), while offset survives in small admin tables where random page access matters more than correctness.

questions.question: A client needs live updates from the server (e.g. a chat or a dashboard). Compare polling, long polling, server-sent events and WebSockets — when is each good enough?
What responsibilities does an API gateway typically take on, and what are the risks of letting it accumulate too much logic?
A user double-clicks 'Pay' and the client also retries on timeout. How do you design the payment endpoint so the customer is never charged twice? Explain idempotency keys end to end.
